subject: Alt season, Bitcoin at $80K, and what we're actually watching
preview: BTC dominance is still running the show. Here's what needs to change.
body:
One of the most common questions I got this week: "When is alt season coming?"

Short answer: not yet. And I want to explain why so you can stop guessing and start reading the data.

Bitcoin dominance is sitting around 59-60%. For a genuine alt season to kick in, we historically need that number down in the low 40s. We are nowhere near that. The Altcoin Index is bouncing between 18 and 49, which tells you the market is undecided at best and Bitcoin-focused at worst.

Here is the other thing people miss. The ETF flows that drove Bitcoin's run are not rotating into altcoins. That money is institutional. It is allocated to Bitcoin specifically. There is no mechanism for a Blackrock Bitcoin ETF holder to suddenly rotate into small-cap altcoins. That is not how those mandates work.

So if you are sitting on altcoin bags waiting for "the rotation," understand that this cycle's structure might look different from 2021. The money coming in is different. The buyers are different. The flow patterns are different.

Does that mean alts will never run? No. But it means waiting for Bitcoin dominance to actually break down before loading up is a much smarter play than front-running something that has not started.

Now, Bitcoin itself.

We need a clean break above $80K. That is the level I am watching. Below that, $68K is the major support zone that has held multiple times. If we lose $68K on a weekly close, the picture changes meaningfully.

April and May are interesting months. We are watching whale accumulation cycles closely. Large holders have been active, and the pattern from previous cycles suggests this window matters for establishing the next directional move.

But here is the honest take: Bitcoin needs to prove it can hold above $80K before I get excited about anything else in the market. If Bitcoin is not confirming strength, everything downstream (alts, narratives, AI tokens) is just noise.

What does this mean for you?

Do not chase alt season before it arrives. Watch BTC dominance. Watch the $80K level. Let the market tell you when conditions have changed rather than trying to predict the turn.

The members who do best in these moments are the ones who stay patient and let the data lead. That has not changed in any cycle I have been through.

subject: Cash, DCA, and why we skip the Bitcoin proxies
preview: How to think about deploying capital, staying disciplined, and avoiding unnecessary complexity.
body:
Three questions came up repeatedly this week, and they are all connected: How should I deploy my cash into Bitcoin? Should I DCA $50 a week? What about buying MicroStrategy instead?

Let me take them one at a time.

Deploying cash into Bitcoin.

If you have a lump sum sitting on the sideline, the approach I recommend is tranches. Do not go all in at once. Do not try to time the perfect entry. Split it into portions and deploy over weeks or months.

More importantly, keep a cash buffer. The single worst position you can be in during a volatile market is being forced to sell because you need the money. That is how people lock in losses at the worst possible time. Your cash buffer is not lazy money. It is your ability to stay in the game.

DCA at $50 a week.

Yes. Full stop.

I do not care if you think $50 is too small. The amount is not the point. The discipline is the point. Most people do not fail because they picked the wrong asset. They fail because they quit accumulating during drawdowns. They buy when it feels good and stop when it feels bad, which is exactly backwards.

$50 a week into Bitcoin, consistently, through drawdowns and rallies, will put you ahead of 90% of people in this space. Not because of the dollar amount, but because you are building a habit that survives market cycles.

The hardest part of DCA is not starting. It is continuing when Bitcoin drops 30% and your portfolio is red. That is when the strategy actually works, because you are buying cheaper. But it does not feel like it is working. That gap between feeling and reality is where most people give up.

Now, MicroStrategy (MSTR).

I get why people are drawn to it. It feels like leveraged Bitcoin exposure through a stock. But here is the problem.

MSTR carries dilution risk. They issue shares to buy Bitcoin, which means your ownership gets diluted over time. The premium to NAV compresses in bear markets, meaning you can lose more than Bitcoin does on the way down. And it correlates with equity markets, so you are adding stock market risk on top of Bitcoin risk.

If you want Bitcoin exposure, just buy Bitcoin. Hold it yourself. Remove the counterparty risk, the equity correlation, and the management decisions you cannot control.

Simplicity is underrated in this space. Direct Bitcoin ownership, regular accumulation, and a cash buffer you do not touch. That is a portfolio strategy that actually survives contact with reality.

subject: Chainlink, TAO, HYPE, and the ETF question: honest takes
preview: The assets you keep asking about. Here is where we actually stand on each one.
body:
Every Q&A session, the same names come up. Chainlink, TAO, HYPE, and the ETH vs SOL ETF question. So let me give you our honest position on each.

Chainlink (LINK)

Chainlink is real infrastructure. It is not vapourware. The oracle network it provides is genuinely important to how decentralised applications work. I respect the technology.

But LINK is down 80% from its all-time high. And Collective Shift does not hold it. Why? Because respecting the technology and allocating capital are two different decisions. We see better risk-to-reward opportunities elsewhere. Being good technology does not automatically make something a good investment at every price point. We need both the fundamentals and the setup to align.

TAO (Bittensor)

The AI and digital asset crossover narrative is one of the loudest in the market right now. TAO sits at the centre of that. It is volatile, it is speculative, and the community is passionate.

Our position: we are not chasing it. Not until Bitcoin confirms its direction. When BTC is range-bound or uncertain, speculative plays like TAO carry amplified risk. If Bitcoin breaks out cleanly and the broader market confirms strength, we can revisit. But buying volatile narrative tokens while Bitcoin has not decided what it is doing is how people end up holding bags.

ETH vs SOL ETFs

This is a structural question more than a trading question. The Ethereum ETFs have roughly $19 billion in AUM. The Solana ETF products are growing but are about a year behind in terms of institutional adoption and flow.

Both are worth watching, but understand the maturity gap. Ethereum ETFs have established flow patterns. Solana ETFs are earlier stage. If you are thinking about exposure through ETF products, Ethereum has the more developed infrastructure today. That could change, but that is where things stand right now.

HYPE (Hyperliquid)

This is one we actually hold. Our average entry is $31.18. We are watching the $30 level closely because that is where the protocol's buyback mechanism provides support. If it holds $30, the thesis stays intact. If we get a deeper pullback to $25, that becomes interesting for adding.

HYPE is a position where the protocol fundamentals (revenue, buybacks, user growth) back up the price action. That is the kind of setup we look for. Not hype, not narrative, but actual cash flows supporting the token.

The common thread across all of these: we do not allocate based on excitement. We allocate based on risk-adjusted opportunity relative to what Bitcoin is doing. Bitcoin leads. Everything else follows. When members ask "should I buy X?" the first question is always "what is Bitcoin doing?" If you do not have a clear answer to that, nothing else matters yet.
